did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning architectures (Unet, Unet++, DeepLabV3, DeepLabV3++, and FPN) to perform skin lesion segmentation on a hybrid dataset of public (ISIC) and private dermoscopy images for dermatological diagnosis and skin cancer detection.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'RESEARCH ARTICLE' in the header. It presents original experimental work evaluating deep learning architectures (Unet, Unet++, DeepLabV3, DeepLabV3++, and FPN) on a hybrid dataset of public ISIC and private clinical images. The paper includes specific methodology, four distinct test scenarios, quantitative results (IoU, Dice, Sensitivity, Specificity, Accuracy), and a conclusion based on these new findings, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: An Evaluation of Skin Lesion Segmentation Using Deep Learning Architectures
journal: Sakarya University Journal of Computer and Information Sciences
brief_summary: This study evaluated the effectiveness of five deep learning segmentation architectures—Unet, Unet++, DeepLabV3, DeepLabV3++, and FPN—for skin lesion segmentation to aid in early skin cancer diagnosis. The models were trained and tested on a hybrid dataset comprising 3,463 public ISIC images and 765 private clinical dermoscopy images from Sakarya University. The experimental design involved four distinct test scenarios using various combinations of public and private data for training and testing to assess generalizability. Performance was quantitatively assessed using metrics including IoU, Dice coefficient, sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy. The results indicated that mixed datasets, which combined public and private data, yielded the best segmentation performance. Specifically, the FPN and DeepLabV3+ models demonstrated superior results across critical metrics, highlighting the benefit of data diversity for improving model robustness.

all_task_types.target: Pixel-wise binary mask delineating the boundaries of skin lesions against the surrounding healthy skin.
all_task_types.details: The paper evaluates deep learning architectures (Unet, Unet++, DeepLabV3, DeepLabV3++, and FPN) for skin lesion segmentation. The models predict a segmentation mask (binary classification at the pixel level: lesion vs. background) rather than a diagnostic class label for the entire image. Performance is measured using IoU, Dice coefficient, sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy based on the overlap between predicted masks and ground truth masks.

datasets_used.name: ISIC (International Skin Imaging Collaboration) Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public, open-access database of dermoscopic images with ground truth annotations, extended in 2016, 2017, 2018, 2019, and 2020. The study utilized a curated subset of this database, removing recurring images to obtain the final count.

use.training: Used as part of the hybrid dataset for training models in various test scenarios.
use.validation: Used as part of the hybrid dataset for validation in various test scenarios.
use.testing: Used as part of the hybrid dataset for testing in various test scenarios.

datasets_used.name: Sakarya University Training and Research Hospital (SEAH) Private Dataset
datasets_used.description: A private collection of high-resolution (1024x1024 pixels) dermoscopic images collected from Sakarya University Training and Research Hospital with ethics committee permission. Ground truth masks were manually determined by a dermatologist.

use.training: Used as part of the hybrid dataset for training models in various test scenarios.
use.validation: Used as part of the hybrid dataset for validation in various test scenarios.
use.testing: Used as part of the hybrid dataset for testing in various test scenarios.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between architectures and datasets were based on descriptive statistics (means and per-scenario values) of metrics such as IoU, Dice coefficient, sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy.

Scenario 1 (Public Train / Private Test).description: Models trained on public ISIC dataset, tested on private Sakarya University dataset.
Scenario 1 (Public Train / Private Test).notes: DeepLabV3 achieved the highest IoU (0.7624) and Dice (0.8636). DeepLabV3 and FPN achieved the highest Specificity (0.9632).

Scenario 2 (Hybrid Train / Hybrid Test).description: Models trained and tested on a hybrid dataset combining public ISIC and private Sakarya University images.
Scenario 2 (Hybrid Train / Hybrid Test).notes: FPN achieved the highest Dice coefficient (0.8939). DeepLabV3+ achieved the highest Accuracy (0.9581). The paper notes that mixed datasets yielded the best results overall.

Scenario 3 (Private Train / Private Test.description: Models trained and tested solely on the private Sakarya University dataset.
Scenario 3 (Private Train / Private Test.notes: Unet achieved the highest IoU (0.8075) and Specificity (0.9641). Unet++ achieved the highest Sensitivity (0.9585). FPN achieved the highest Dice (0.8874). DeepLabV3+ achieved the highest Accuracy (0.9564).

Scenario 4 (Public Train / Public Test).description: Models trained and tested solely on the public ISIC dataset.
Scenario 4 (Public Train / Public Test).notes: Unet++ achieved the highest IoU (0.8229) and Dice (0.8859). Unet achieved the highest Accuracy (0.9517).

Average Performance (Across All Scenario.description: Average metrics across all four experimental scenarios.
Average Performance (Across All Scenario.notes: Unet achieved the highest average IoU (0.7995), Dice (0.8763), Sensitivity (0.9329), Specificity (0.9594), and Accuracy (0.9519).

Comparison with Existing Studies.description: Comparison of the proposed system's best results (Hybrid FPN) with other recent studies.
Comparison with Existing Studies.notes: The proposed system outperformed studies relying solely on public datasets in the hybrid scenario. Study [33] reported higher metrics on ISIC 2016/2017 but used different datasets.

architecture_used.main_model: Unet, Unet++, DeepLabV3, DeepLabV3++, and Feature Pyramid Network (FPN)

architecture_details.Unet: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with a symmetric encoder-decoder structure and skip connections.
architecture_details.Unet++: Modified Unet with nested, dense skip pathways to reduce the semantic gap between encoder and decoder feature maps.
architecture_details.DeepLabV3: Encoder-decoder structure using dilated convolutions and Atrous Spatial Pyramid Pooling (ASPP) to capture multi-scale context.
architecture_details.DeepLabV3+: Uses DeepLabV3 encoder module and atrous convolution to adjust feature resolution, with an ASPP module to extract contextual information from multi-scale receptive fields.
architecture_details.FPN: Feature Pyramid Network architecture designed to handle scale variations in lesions.

training_details.hardware: NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3060 12GB GPU, AMD Ryzen 2700 CPU, 32GB RAM

classification_or_output_head.details: Outputs a binary mask delineating lesion boundaries against healthy skin.

preprocessing_techniques: Removal of recurring images from the curated ISIC database to obtain the final dataset count.
Data augmentation during training: horizontal flipping, vertical flipping, random rotation, and brightness adjustment.

conclusions: Mixed datasets combining public ISIC and private clinical data yielded the best segmentation performance across all evaluated deep learning architectures.

The Feature Pyramid Network (FPN) architecture demonstrated superior segmentation quality, particularly in challenging cases where lesions closely resemble surrounding tissue, achieving the highest Dice coefficient (0.8939) in the hybrid training/testing scenario.

The use of a curated private dataset with ground truth images provided promising results, supporting the effectiveness of combining public and private data sources to improve model robustness and adaptability.
Model performance varied significantly across different dataset compositions, indicating that data diversity and source-specific characteristics impact segmentation quality.
The proposed hybrid approach outperformed studies relying solely on public datasets in the hybrid scenario, although some existing studies using different datasets reported higher metrics.
important_remarks: The study relies on a relatively small private dataset (765 images) from a single institution (Sakarya University), which limits the generalizability of the results to other clinical settings and populations.
Ground truth masks for the private dataset were manually determined by a single dermatologist, introducing potential annotation bias and lack of inter-observer variability assessment.

The paper does not report any formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals; performance comparisons are based solely on descriptive statistics (means and per-scenario values).
Data leakage risk exists due to the use of a curated ISIC dataset where recurring images were removed, but the specific deduplication method and impact on data distribution are not fully detailed.

The hybrid dataset approach (combining public ISIC and private clinical data) yielded the best performance, suggesting that data diversity is critical for robustness, but the specific contribution of each data source to the final metrics is not isolated.

The study evaluates only five specific deep learning architectures (Unet, Unet++, DeepLabV3, DeepLabV3++, FPN) and does not compare against more recent transformer-based models or state-of-the-art architectures beyond those mentioned in related work.

No external validation was performed; the model was tested on the same private dataset source it was partially trained on (in hybrid scenarios), limiting the assessment of true out-of-distribution generalization.

The paper acknowledges that the private dataset includes challenging cases (dark skin tones, lesions covering the entire field, lesions resembling skin color), but the performance on these specific subgroups is not broken down in the results.
